item.title: Charters' court ruling highlights serious flaws and failures in how FICAC acted in charging him and breaching his constitutional rights
item.description: The permanent stay order on the case of Fijian and British national, Charlie Charters by High Court Judge, Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu can be described as a landmark ruling for the country. Charters was charged with two counts of aiding and abetting after allegedly helping a FICAC officer unlawfully publish confidential FICAC information.
item.content:encoded:
The permanent stay order on the case of Fijian and British national, Charlie Charters by High Court Judge, Justice Pita Bulamainaivalu can be described as a landmark ruling for the country.

Charters was charged with two counts of aiding and abetting after allegedly helping a FICAC officer unlawfully publish confidential FICAC information.

Charters was stopped at Nadi International Airport on the morning of Saturday 21st February 2026, driven down by FICAC officers to Suva, taken to the FICAC office in the night, held in custody on Saturday and Sunday, and then charged and presented in court on Monday 23rd February.

The judge ruled today that Charters' right to remain silent was violated by FICAC, Charters' right to freedom of speech, expression, thought, opinion and publication under the 2013 Constitution including not to divulge the sources of his journalistic information was violated by FICAC, there was abuse of process by FICAC, the Commission's case on Counts 1 and 2 of aiding and abetting against Charters will inevitably or doomed to fail, and there is a conflict of interest with regard to count 2.

item.title: Education officer faces action after arranging church group's school visit without Ministry approval
item.description: A Ministry of Education officer is set to face disciplinary action after facilitating a visit by a church group to Natabua High School without the Ministry's approval. The development comes after a video circulated on social media showing a member of the visiting group appearing to single out a student because of his faith during a school session that happened this week.
item.content:encoded:
A Ministry of Education officer is set to face disciplinary action after facilitating a visit by a church group to Natabua High School without the Ministry's approval.

The development comes after a video circulated on social media showing a member of the visiting group appearing to single out a student because of his faith during a school session that happened this week.

Education Minister Aseri Radrodro says he has directed Permanent Secretary for Education, Navin Raj to take the necessary action against the education officer who arranged the visit without following the Ministry's approval process.

He says the Ministry's approval procedures exist to safeguard students and ensure all activities conducted in schools are appropriate, respectful and consistent with Fiji's education policies.

The Minister says an investigation will establish the full circumstances surrounding the incident, determine whether any Ministry policies or procedures were breached, and identify any appropriate administrative action.

The Ministry has also confirmed that the visiting Steadfast Baptist Church group consisted of members from Houston, Texas, in the United States, together with a United States national residing in the Philippines.

Radrodro also met representatives of the United States Embassy this morning to discuss the matter.

According to the Ministry, embassy officials confirmed they had no prior knowledge of, or involvement in, the group's visit or activities in Fiji.

The Ministry is reminding organisations and individuals that prior written approval must be obtained before conducting activities or engaging with students on school premises.

They also say parental consent is required where activities involve photography, filming or the publication of students' images.

The investigation follows widespread concern over a video circulating on social media that appears to show Christian preacher and content creator Osaslugo addressing students at Natabua High School before calling attention to a student identified as Muslim and telling him that because he does not follow Jesus Christ or Christianity, he will not go to heaven.

The footage has sparked criticism online, with many questioning how the preacher was allowed to address students during school hours and whether the visit had been approved.

A parent whose child attended the session told fijivillage News that families were neither informed nor asked for consent before the preacher addressed students and only became aware of the incident after the video appeared online.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Dreams realised as new lawyers admitted to the bar
item.description: University of Fiji student Avishek Pawan Nair has fulfilled his dream of becoming a lawyer after being admitted to the bar at the Suva High Court, dedicating the achievement to his late father, whose death during his first year of law school nearly ended his legal journey. Originally from Nadi, Nair says studying law during the COVID-19 pandemic was particularly challenging as classes moved online
item.content:encoded:
University of Fiji student Avishek Pawan Nair has fulfilled his dream of becoming a lawyer after being admitted to the bar at the Suva High Court, dedicating the achievement to his late father, whose death during his first year of law school nearly ended his legal journey.

Originally from Nadi, Nair says studying law during the COVID-19 pandemic was particularly challenging as classes moved online, while relocating from Nadi to Suva to complete his Graduate Diploma in Legal Practice also proved difficult.

Meanwhile, inspired by a high school teacher who always told her that there should be justice, Divya Darsana Raj has realised her ambition of joining Fiji's legal profession.

Originally from Lautoka, Raj spoke to fijivillage News moments after the admission ceremony and admitted she was overwhelmed with emotion.

Raj says her passion for law began in high school, where a teacher inspired her to pursue the profession.

She also thanked her family and friends for supporting her throughout her studies, saying that although her parents were unable to attend the admission ceremony, her fiancé was by her side to celebrate the achievement.

Also admitted to the High Court was Ifrana Zoya Ali, who is originally from Nadi and now lives in Sigatoka.

Ali says she had always wanted to study criminology, but financial and family circumstances meant she could not pursue it overseas.

She says law became the closest path to achieving her dream of working in criminal law and family law.

Ali says the journey was not easy, as she had to leave her family and move to Suva for five months to complete her Graduate Diploma in Legal Practice.

She says adjusting to life in Suva was challenging, but the unwavering support of her family, particularly her mother and brothers, helped her through the difficult period.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: No-case-to-answer application in Rewa Street and Flagstaff meth case set for Aug 4th
item.description: The no-case-to-answer hearing in the Rewa Street and Flagstaff methamphetamine case has been scheduled for August 4th. Defence lawyers Maria Raga and Shirley Tivou submitted the no-case-to-answer application today while Jerry Dinati was present in court. Magistrate Sufia Hamza has given Dinati time until close of business to make the application. She has given prosecution lawyer, Eoghn Samisoni ti
item.content:encoded:
The no-case-to-answer hearing in the Rewa Street and Flagstaff methamphetamine case has been scheduled for August 4th. Defence lawyers Maria Raga and Shirley Tivou submitted the no-case-to-answer application today while Jerry Dinati was present in court. Magistrate Sufia Hamza has given Dinati time until close of business to make the application.

She has given prosecution lawyer, Eoghn Samisoni time until next Friday to respond to the application. It is alleged that Josua Ravula was found with unlawful possession of 42.295 grams of methamphetamine. Yu Yang Feng, Minghui Dai and Zhou Lan are each charged with one count of unlawful possession of illicit drugs for allegedly being found with 39.509 grams of methamphetamine. Lan is also charged with possession of property suspected to be proceeds of a crime for allegedly being found with FJD$7,180, USD$60, 1,520 in Hong Kong currency and 1,600 Chinese yuan. Feng is also charged with unlawful supply of illicit drugs. It is alleged that on the 23rd of September, 2025, without lawful authority, Feng engaged in dealings with other persons for the transport and supply of methamphetamine. He is also charged with being in possession of property suspected to be proceeds of a crime. Feng was allegedly found with $906.25 in cash. Choi and Peniasi Delai are charged with one count of unlawful possession of illicit drugs for allegedly being found with 0.345 grams of methamphetamine.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Charters - the court ruling is a win for journalism in Fiji, I'm delighted that the burden of the FICAC prosecution has been lifted off me
Grandmother convicted over grandson’s death, gets suspended jail sentence
item.description: Susana Radinivugalei has been convicted of one count of manslaughter arising from a breach of duty and sentenced to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, over the death of her grandson who was in her care. On the 29th January this year, Radinivugalei was taking two of her grandchildren, including Elijah Jibola, to school along Mission Road in Sigatoka.
item.content:encoded:
Susana Radinivugalei has been convicted of one count of manslaughter arising from a breach of duty and sentenced to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, over the death of her grandson who was in her care.

On the 29th January this year, Radinivugalei was taking two of her grandchildren, including Elijah Jibola, to school along Mission Road in Sigatoka.

She crossed the road at a pedestrian crossing in Sigatoka Town with her elder grandchild, leaving Elijah about 10 metres behind.

Radinivugalei and the elder child reached the other side safely, while Elijah attempted to cross the road on his own he was hit by a car.

He was rushed to Sigatoka Hospital, where he was pronounced dead on arrival.

Radinivugalei admitted her negligence and pleaded guilty to the charge of manslaughter arising from a breach of duty.

Justice Sunil Sharma accepted that she was genuinely remorseful, had fully cooperated with police investigations and had no previous convictions.

Justice Sharma says that Radinivugalei had already suffered the devastating loss of her grandson and continues to care for her other grandchildren.

However, Justice Sharma stressed that the offence was serious and that the tragedy could have been avoided had the child not been left unattended, adding that no amount of repentance could bring the child back.

As part of the sentence, Radinivugalei must complete two sessions of general and parental counselling through the Ministry of Women, Children and Social Protection within three months. Failure to comply with the order could result in committal proceedings.

Meanwhile, the driver, Komal Sapna Singh has pleaded not guilty to a charge of manslaughter.

Her case has been adjourned to 4th August for a pre-trial conference, and her bail has been further extended.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.description: An asphalt milling machine blocking a public footpath along Selbourne Street in Suva has raised safety concerns, particularly for
